title: Bracketed Template Validator
description_md: A configuration template may contain three kinds of paired markers: (), [], and {}. A template is valid if every opener is closed by the same type, closures happen in the correct order, and no closer appears without a matching opener. Return true if the template is valid and false otherwise.

examples.explanation: Each opener is closed by the correct marker in reverse order.
The closing order does not match the most recent opener.

canonical_solution_md: Use a stack of opening markers and a lookup dict that maps each closer to its expected opener: {')': '(', ']': '[', '}': '{'}. When you see an opener, push it. When you see a closer, the stack must be non-empty and its top must be the matching opener from the lookup dict. Pop on a match, and return false immediately on any mismatch or empty stack. At the end, the stack must be empty; leftover openers mean the template is invalid.
fallback_hints: When you read a closing marker, which earlier symbol is the only one that matters?
A last-in, first-out structure matches the nesting behavior of brackets.
Push openers on a stack. On each closer, check the top of the stack for the matching opener using a lookup dict, then pop it.
common_mistakes: Only counting totals of each bracket type instead of enforcing order.
Popping from an empty stack on an early closer.
Forgetting to reject leftover openers after the scan finishes.
follow_up_questions: Why is counting the number of open and close symbols not enough?
How would you extend the validator to ignore bracket characters that appear inside quoted strings?
